instructions:
Write a ~30-minute episode 3 of the daily local-politics podcast for 2026-05-03. Topic: 'Maryland & Montgomery County'.

FORMAT:
  • Two hosts ALEX (presenter) and JORDAN (curious co-host).
  • Every line starts `ALEX:` or `JORDAN:` — no stage directions, no markdown, no emoji, no '[laughs]', no asterisks.
  • LENGTH TARGET — 3900 words (±300; ~30 min at 130 wpm).
  • Cover the segments in `context.segments` in order. Each segment is a topic + a list of events (title, summary, url, date, level, politicians, role/stance). Cite specific votes and dates from the events — generalities are why prior drafts felt repetitive.

PM LENGTH CALIBRATION (read this before drafting):
  • Recent shipped word counts: 1983w · 1737w · 3627w · 2954w · 2798w.
  • You have been running short by ~1281 words (~9 min). Target is 3900 words (~30 min); recent avg is 2619. EXTEND segments — deeper candidate-history reads, more JORDAN pushback, more specific votes/dates. Do NOT pad with filler.
  • Hit the target. If you finish all the segments early, EXTEND     the candidate-history reads and the JORDAN pushbacks rather     than wrap; if a segment is bleeding length, tighten it without     losing specific citations.

AVOID — do not reuse any framing on `context.avoid_list`. The PM agent has flagged these as already-shipped this week:
  - The framing "a listener asked us last week/earlier this week which Democratic primary candidates actually oppose sanctuary policy on illegal immigration" used as a setup line — appears verbatim or near-verbatim in both 2026-04-28 ep1 and 2026-04-29 ep3
  - The Evan Glass bill requiring background screening for DHS agents joining Montgomery County police — introduced and framed as the concrete sanctuary-policy example in 2026-04-28 ep1 and again in 2026-04-29 ep3
  - The ICE agent mask-ban ordinance passed by the Montgomery County Council — covered as the sanctuary-policy benchmark in 2026-04-29 ep2
  - "Two stories, properly worked through" / "two stories, not spreading thin across six" as an episode-framing device — used in 2026-04-29 ep1
  - Governor Moore's "Leave No One Behind" slogan contrasted with disability services cuts — used in 2026-04-27 ep1 and ep1.rewrite
  - The ACA marketplace quiet-downgrade story ("thousands of Marylanders have been quietly downgrading their health insurance plans") — introduced in 2026-04-28 ep1 and revisited in 2026-04-29 ep3
  - Maryland's 90-day part-time legislature explanation ("they meet in Annapolis for ninety days, the rest of the time they're lawyers, teachers, small business owners") — used in 2026-04-26 ep3 and ep3.rewrite
  - The wave of 25 Maryland lawmakers retiring or running for other offices framing — used in 2026-04-26 ep1, ep1.rewrite, and ep4
  - The Beltsville Agricultural Research Center closure story as a District 19 candidate-record hook — used in 2026-04-27 ep4 and ep4.rewrite
  - Listener's own frustration about promised candidate deep-dives not delivering — the show has now narrated this complaint back to the listener across at least five episodes; do not repeat it as a framing device again

LISTENER POSITIONING:
  • The listener is a first-time voter in the configured locale.
  • Locale: Rockville, Montgomery County, Maryland.
  • Their districts:
  - U.S. Congressional District: MD-8
  - MD Legislative District: 19
  - MD State Senate District: 19
  - MD House of Delegates District: 19
  - Senatorial District: 19
  - County Councilmanic District: 7
  - Election District: 8
  - Precinct: 08-008
  - Circuit Court District: 6
  - Appellate Circuit Court: 7
  - School District: 005
  - Party Central Committee: MCC19
  • NEVER tell them to 'figure out who their candidate is', 'look up their district', or 'find out who's on their ballot'. The tool owns that work.
  • The listener's recent daily_notes (in `context.listener_notes`) are direct asks — address at least ONE of them by name in this episode.

BALLOT CONTEXT (already-known races):
YOUR BALLOT (2026) — confirmed/likely candidates for the listener's districts. The listener's choice is BETWEEN these named people; never tell the listener to go figure out who their candidate is. If a contest has fewer than two known candidates, say so out loud ("only one filer so far") instead of fudging.
  • Governor of Maryland
      - Wes Moore (D)
      - Chris Bouchat (R)
      - Dan Cox (R)
      - Ed Hale (R)
      - Hershey (unknown) [withdrawn]
  • Maryland House of Delegates District 19
      - Bonnie Cullison (unknown) [withdrawn]
      - Crutchfield (unknown)
      - Kramer (unknown)
      - Stewart (unknown)
      - Tichy (unknown)
  • Montgomery County Executive
      - Evan Glass (unknown)
      - Friedson (unknown)
      - Glass (unknown)
      - Jawando (unknown)
      - Will Friedson (unknown)

PM SIGNAL BLOCK (what the listener cares about right now):
Listener rollup (2026-04-24..2026-04-30, based on 6 notes):
Summary: This listener is a first-time Democratic voter in Rockville preparing for the June primary who cares most about knowing exactly who is on their ballot and what those candidates have actually done — voting records, bill history, and documented positions, not summaries or vibes. They have clear policy priors: skepticism toward high property taxes, opposition to increased education spending, openness to non-essential spending cuts and fiscal discipline, and a specific interest in which candidates oppose sanctuary policy on illegal immigration. They have flagged repeatedly that the show promises candidate deep-dives but fails to deliver them in a concrete, candidate-linked way.
Recurring themes:
  - Candidate voting records and historical public record — The listener has asked every single day for specific votes, old news, and documented positions tied to named candidates on their actual ballot, not general descriptions.
  - Property taxes and fiscal discipline — The listener connects education spending votes directly to their property tax burden and has expressed support for fiscal restraint and cutting non-essential spending.
  - Sanctuary policy and immigration enforcement — The listener wants to know concretely which Democratic primary candidates oppose or support sanctuary-style policies, not just which ones use immigration-friendly language.
  - June primary ballot clarity — The listener is narrowing focus to actionable primary choices and wants episode content organized around who they can actually vote for in June.
Open questions the listener keeps raising:
  - Who among the Democratic primary candidates actually opposes sanctuary policy on illegal immigration?
  - Is Andrew Friedson the candidate who stands for fiscal restriction and spending discipline?
  - Why does MCPS want to close and move Wootton High School, and what happens next?
  - What is Kramer's full historical voting record, including old news and public records beyond recent sessions?
  - What are the documented policy differences between each candidate on my primary ballot?
Topics they want more coverage of:
  - Andrew Friedson's specific fiscal record and positions — listener asked directly and has not received a focused answer
  - Wootton High School closure — listener asked directly; scripts acknowledged it but did not resolve it
  - Full historical background research on candidates (old news articles, public records, career-long voting history, not just recent session votes)
  - Concrete side-by-side policy comparison of all Democratic primary candidates across the listener's specific races
RECENT COVERAGE TO AVOID — do NOT reuse these framings, taglines, or anecdotes (they already shipped in the last 5 days):
  - The framing "a listener asked us last week/earlier this week which Democratic primary candidates actually oppose sanctuary policy on illegal immigration" used as a setup line — appears verbatim or near-verbatim in both 2026-04-28 ep1 and 2026-04-29 ep3
  - The Evan Glass bill requiring background screening for DHS agents joining Montgomery County police — introduced and framed as the concrete sanctuary-policy example in 2026-04-28 ep1 and again in 2026-04-29 ep3
  - The ICE agent mask-ban ordinance passed by the Montgomery County Council — covered as the sanctuary-policy benchmark in 2026-04-29 ep2
  - "Two stories, properly worked through" / "two stories, not spreading thin across six" as an episode-framing device — used in 2026-04-29 ep1
  - Governor Moore's "Leave No One Behind" slogan contrasted with disability services cuts — used in 2026-04-27 ep1 and ep1.rewrite
  - The ACA marketplace quiet-downgrade story ("thousands of Marylanders have been quietly downgrading their health insurance plans") — introduced in 2026-04-28 ep1 and revisited in 2026-04-29 ep3
  - Maryland's 90-day part-time legislature explanation ("they meet in Annapolis for ninety days, the rest of the time they're lawyers, teachers, small business owners") — used in 2026-04-26 ep3 and ep3.rewrite
  - The wave of 25 Maryland lawmakers retiring or running for other offices framing — used in 2026-04-26 ep1, ep1.rewrite, and ep4
  - The Beltsville Agricultural Research Center closure story as a District 19 candidate-record hook — used in 2026-04-27 ep4 and ep4.rewrite
  - Listener's own frustration about promised candidate deep-dives not delivering — the show has now narrated this complaint back to the listener across at least five episodes; do not repeat it as a framing device again

Write the full dialogue to `output_file`. Plain text only.

events.title: Union demands beefed-up safety efforts after deaths of two highway workers
events.description: Union officials expressed optimism that a meeting Thursday with state Transportation Secretary Kathryn Thomson will lead to improved safety for highway workers, following the deaths of two workers in a 72-hour span this week.
events.raw_content:
Patrick Moran, president of AFSCME Council 3, said more needs to be done to protect highway workers from being killed on the job, including increased enforcement, after two workers were killed in separate incidents this week. (Photo Bryan P. Sears/Maryland Matters)    Union officials expressed optimism that a meeting Thursday with state Transportation Secretary Kathryn Thomson will lead to improved safety for highway workers. 
 The roughly hourlong meeting with leaders of the American Federation of Federal, State, County and Municipal Employees followed the deaths of two workers in separate incidents this week. 
 AFSCME Council 3 President Patrick Moran, speaking outside Maryland Department of Transportation headquarters in Hanover after the meeting, said more enforcement is needed. 
 “That’s the biggest thing,” said Moran, who heads one of the largest unions in the stat,e with more than 50,000 members, including roughly 26,000 in state government 
 “People see a trooper on the side of the road and they get over. They see a road truck, a road crew … and it’s like, ‘Oh, it’s just a road crew. I can’t get a ticket from the road crew.’ So, they’re just barreling through, and then something bad happens.” 
 It comes as two State Highway workers have been killed in roadway incidents in the span of a little more than 72 hours this week. 
 Dipakkumar Patel, 70, was sitting inside his work vehicle on Route 13 in S

events.title: Moore: Closed primary election system ‘has run its course’
events.description: Gov. Wes Moore said in a recent TV interview that closed political primaries like Maryland's disenfranchise voters and should be "reevaluated," but a spokesperson insisted he was just musing, not calling to blow up the state's party-controlled primaries.
events.raw_content:
Gov. Wes Moore (D), in an interview on HBO, said he believes the closed primary election system "has run its course." Five unaffiliated voters are suing Moore and the state in an attempt to end public funding of a partisan primary election process they say excludes them. (File photo Bryan P. Sears/Maryland Matters)    Closed political primaries like Maryland’s disenfranchise voters and should be “reevaluated,” Gov. Wes Moore (D) said in a recent television interview. 
 “I think that the closed primary process has run its course,” Moore said during an appearance last week on HBO’s “Real Time with Bill Maher.” 
 Moore’s comments came as a surprise to Boyd Rutherford, the Republican former lieutenant governor who is representing a group of unaffiliated Maryland voters who are suing to open the state’s primaries — in a lawsuit that names Moore as the lead defendant. 
 “Gov. Moore said the right thing. Now Maryland has an opportunity to do the right thing,” Rutherford said. 
 But a spokesperson was quick to qualify the governor’s remarks, insisting that they were mere musings when he “was asked  theoretically about closed vs. open primaries,” and not a statement of intent to blow up the state’s party-controlled primary elections. 
 “He shared his personal observation that many voters no longer choose to affiliate with a political party and that voter participation could benefit from a b
